lenses.guidance: Narrative irony lands in the Hebrew of v 25: 'why have you ramah me?' — Laban's deception of Jacob uses the same root as Esau's complaint about Jacob in 27:35. The deceiver is deceived; the verb plotted against Esau now turns on Jacob. The text indicts him by echo, no comment.

Despite the chaos of Laban's swap (vv 21-30), the line of seed advances through Leah — not the favored Rachel. Verses 31-35 introduce Reuben, Simeon, Levi, and Judah. The redemptive line to David and to Christ runs through the unloved wife (Mt 1:2-3).

Leah's son Judah (v 35) becomes the canonical channel: Jacob's blessing names him 'a lion's cub' from whom 'the scepter will not depart' (49:9-10), and Matthew 1 traces Christ's line through him. The Lion of Judah of Revelation 5:5 is born from this loveless triangle.
